narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 41.6% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from details like your undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, year-one and year-three school deciles both at 3, middle family income bracket, and full-time enrollment plans. Among the key elements analyzed, your undergraduate GPA stood out as the most influential positive factor, as your 3.20 is very close to the 3.24 average seen in students who passed the bar, nudging the model's view toward expecting success rather than failure. Next in importance was your middle family income level, which also worked in your favor—it's a common bracket among bar passers (about 35% of them), helping to counterbalance other concerns and push the prediction away from failure. However, your year-three decile of 3 had the opposite effect, being lower than the 5.91 average for those who passed, which pulled the overall assessment toward a higher risk of failing the bar. Your LSAT score of 30, well below the 37.27 average of successful students, and year-one decile of 3 (versus 6.04 average) added to those concerns, though they weren't the top drivers here. Compared to typical admitted students—who often have stronger deciles, higher LSATs, and similar GPAs or incomes—your profile aligns well on GPA and income but falls short on test scores and class rankings, tipping the balance against you. While factors like being full-time (the norm for 93% of passers) helped, they couldn't overcome the gaps. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—gender and race weren't considered at all—but a data-driven estimate to help the school select applicants most likely to thrive. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the typical 37+, strengthening your class deciles through additional strong performance, or exploring programs with more holistic reviews. You might also connect with advisors for bar prep strategies tailored to students with similar starting points, as many have turned around predictions like this through targeted improvement. Keep pushing; your solid GPA shows you have the foundation to build on.
